item.content:encoded: Really cool idea. Like the different ideas for playing around with physics and science. Don't know much about the Church of the Second Hytoth but I'm familiar enough with the basic concept so see how it's relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi Patrick, how the [Dolphin whistle] are ya'?
seriously though this is super weird and interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't think I would learn about physics reading the scp wiki.
I was wrong

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic weird physics skip. I'm on the fence with the final transmission, but it'd have to be terrible to ruin my previous enjoyment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Outside of the Ortothan (and Pattern Screamer) connections, I still don’t get how this fits into the Ad-Astra canon. Could someone clue me in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason this reminded me more of the film Annihilation than it did of 319.

Edit: also, the film Arrival.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this article. Usually I'm a bit put off by really technical skips because I'm not too bright, but this was written in a very easy to understand and educational way, so great job with that.

I'm probably way off base, but my current theory is that Yesodon made our false vacuum universe and its set of constants, leaving -2 trapped in the chaotic desolate remnant of his true vacuum universe. Humans, having grown and thrived in the false vacuum, are seen by the being as its killer, and it is very upset that we're here staring at it. Seems like it has plans to expand and take us out someday, when Yesodon is out of the picture.

Of course, this theory may change or be completely scrapped as I dive more into related SCPs and canons, but it was still fun to think about just from what's shown in the article. Great job again, very intriguing read!
It's about time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm…not entirely sure I get this. I understand the whole idea of false vacuums and metastability from 319, but I don't quite get what else there is to the scip other than Fucky Physics Zone - and the garbled mistranslated transmission felt generic enough to detract from the thing rather than add to it, especially if you're going from hard sci-fi. I want to understand it, I just don't get where you're going with it - downvote for now, I'm afraid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When you realize Randall Munroe wrote something uncannily similar to this skip months before 2821 was posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting idea. It would have to be a slightly-less-false vacuum, then, rather than a true vacuum — otherwise it would only be single-use thing. That diagram in the footnote would have to be a lot bumpier.

Additionally, since one of these bubbles can only expand at the speed of light anyways, this alien civilization would want to be pretty close by, since it can only expand at the speed of light, and the universe expands a bit faster than that on the wide scale. Why put it in our moon if they weren't in the neighborhood?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My interpretation: this is a higher-tech civilization's attempt at SCP-2000, but only better. In case the horrible happens, we built a reinforced bunker with Scranton anchors - they built a fucking true vacuum and somehow managed to move their beings across the border into it. All else goes wrong for us, we clear the Earth and methodically repopulate it, for them, they let the bubble loose and wipe out the universe entirely, and start with a clean slate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note that the diameter in this is referring to the anomaly itself, not the cavern's diameter, so there's still room around 2821. I've changed the bit about mining equipment, so thanks for pointing that out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When the probes were sent in, the laws of physics were similar enough (in some aspects) to our own that they didn't get completely destroyed. Note that these changed laws of physics can include additions to the laws, not just revisions, and that φ2 was the only set that was this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One minor question: if the anomaly is a true vacuum with different laws of physics, wouldn't most probes sent into it instantly fall apart at the subatomic level?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found a couple of probable typos, and I don't think the corrections are self-evident enough to do them myself. I've underlined them in the quote block:

SCP-2821 was discovered during tunneling from Lunar Area-32 to Lunar Area-13, which would have served as a means of transportation between the two sites. On 13/4/2015, after digging 1.25 km from Area-32, one of the mining equipment fell into a large cavern containing SCP-2821. At this point the cavern had a diameter of 0.8 km, and SCP-2821's size was fluctuating at a rapid rate. During the ensuing investigation and development of containment procedures SCP-2821 reached a diameter of 0.3 km for a day, before reaching 0.9 km, creating the current cavern size and creating a hole to the lunar surface. This investigation led to the current accepted theory of this being a true vacuum.

I think you wanted the diameter to reach 3 km, so there would be plenty of space around the 0.9km vacuum. And perhaps you want "one of the mining systems" or "a piece of mining equipment"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: VK-Class scenario would be like the world ending or some really really bad event. This same nomenclature is used with some other keter class SCP that could destroy reality or the world as we know it if containment is breached.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mostly you could work with foot notes, that was the core of my suggestion. It would clean the text without taking out any of the explanations that forms the basis for the SCP.

It isn't a matter of reducing information, but of arranging it to make a better deliver of both story and the science behind it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those have been fixed! I also plan on doing more similarly realistic scips in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is theorized that our universe exists as a long lived false vacuum on the border of stability

Long-lived is a compound adjective, and thus hyphenated.

For one month various SCP-2821-1 instances resembling nebula

This would be the plural, nebulae.

Other than those two things, this is perfect. I love this type of sci-fi and I love seeing it on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This. The hard science is what makes it scary; true vacuums TERRIFY me. Like, pure, existential dread. I couldn't +1 this hard enough when I saw it finally posted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to add before you started slicing that I disagree completely that you need to cut out the hard science bits and hope you leave them as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I can improve this, which bits do you think could be trimmed down the most?

All things considered so little would likely be shortened that I'm retracting the previous statement. This scip was meant to be hard sci-fi, and I've realized that removing anything would decrease that aspect I intended to have. Feel free to reply with any recommendations for changes, but don't expect them to go through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Disagree. I think you can trim it more, get some more of the science behind it in foot notes. You'll have a hell of a lot of foot notes, but it can add a lot.

I enjoyed it very much, but the excess of physics made it hard to read. When the descriptions of the states of laws of physics inside the anomaly, I was too tired to continue.

No voting for now, but make it simpler to read, and it will be an upvote for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I did enjoy 2460, I felt like it could've offered explanations for some of the physics terms involved (though I've come to understand them over time). I also felt like the end twist of it being dark matter was a bit meh. I do understand that some of the physics exposition in this will be a turn off, though, but there's not much I can really do about that when trying to make the scip realistic while explaining things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I genuinely tried to read through all this, but the dense physics exposition is like a cockslap of information that I wasn't all that compelled or interested in. The last bit at the end is the one bit that sort of drew me in, but the overall piece is too focussed on verisimilitude to sustain my interest and get me reading past the description. It feels like a weaker SCP-2460, without either the snappiness of its description or the gut-punch of its stinger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's because it is based on false vacuum decay, mainly the aftermath of it which would be a true vacuum (with less large scale universe destruction). It's good to know you were able to enjoy it even though some of the physics was confusing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The truth has been revealed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was really engaging from start to finish. The physics help lend a bit of credibility, and you do a fine job of establishing it as a doomsday-level skip even before diving into the addenda, which escalates the weird and creepy sevenfold. My favorite bit is definitely -2's actions in φ20, and the Foundation's reaction to it. Well worth the read, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me a bit about false vacuum decay. Overall, I like this SCP, a bit confusing but good nonetheless.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the foundation has the completely wrong idea here

it's not actually capable of harming the earth
because it's been set to deactivate whenever it gets to close to the earth

someone made it as a proof of concept, and did the sensible thing:

included a safety switch

Foundation has no idea, and is all frantically classifying it as Keter

but it's totally Safe

who made it: we will never know
why was it made: proof of concept
why on the moon: safety! You don't do shit like this on a planet where you live!

if it gets too close to Earth, it switches off

that's why it keeps subsiding

expand expand expand OOPS TOO CLOSE switches off

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All 2417 really adds is the Ortothan language. If I'm going to make up a language, I'm going to use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 319 was in the back of my mind while reading through this, but it didn't have any real bearing on my enjoyment of it. The science seemed legit, the lunar setting was a great choice to increase the lonely, remote mood of the piece, and I was engaged throughout all the differing physics states. I haven't read 2417 yet, so I don't know how much of a difference that makes wrt to Addendum-2, but I hope to remedy that soon. +φ1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I began writing this before I read 319, so no. However these two might have a connection in a project I might be working on. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any relation to SCP-319, as it also uses a true (or at least truer) vacuum as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for physics mindfuckery. Bravo, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is my new scip. Yay. After an imperial fuckton of researching physics stuff, it's finally up. Of course, thanks to everyone that critted this for me (especially to Roget for giving me the idea for a possible plan to contain this thing). Hopefully you guys will be able to understand the physics!

IMAGE CREDITS: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Meta-stability.svg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool, my only thing would be maybe explaining what constitutes a VK-Class scenario in a footnote or something. Nevertheless it was a very thorough explanation of an idea that presents a lot of opportunity to have fun with physics. +1
